articles.title: Boutique Firms Beat The Market — See Also
articles.content: 法庭胜诉与薪资竞赛：钱太多了！Kellogg律所犒赏律师：甚至还有特别奖金！精品律所初级律师起薪25.5万美元！Desmarais LLP大秀慷慨！快速薪酬！审判精品所Elsberg Baker & Maruri与Milbank持平！Wilkinson Stekloff加入薪资盛宴：哇哦哇哦！Seward & Kissel登上薪资列车：干得好！其他律所呢？又见检察官滥用职权：检察官利用警察数据库监视情敌。保守派法学教授感到需要克制：这是言论自由问题还是维持社交的必要代价？ATL法学院排名出炉！看看你的梦想学校（和实际就读的学校）排第几！Above the Law首发：精品律所超越市场——相关阅读。
articles.summary:
Wins In The Courtroom And The Salary Race: So much money!

Kellogg Feeds Their Associates’ Wallets: They even included a special bonus!

Boutique Starts Their First-Years At $255K!: Desmarais LLP is showing off their generosity in a big way!

Quick And Speedy Compensation!: Trial boutique Elsberg Baker & Maruri matches Milbank!

Wilkinson Stekloff Joins The Salary Bonanza: Woop Woop!

Seward & Kissel Joins The Salary Train: Good on them! Now, where’s the rest of the firms?

More Prosecutorial Abuse: Prosecutor used cop database to spy on romantic competition.

Conservative Law Professors Feel The Need To Hold Back: Is this a free speech issue or what it takes to keep things social?

The ATL Law School Ranking Is Live!: See where your dream school (and actual school) place!

articles.title: Formal Law Firm Mentorship Programs Are At Death’s Door
articles.content:
在任何大型律师事务所或政府法律部门，或者至少在过去是这样，每一位新入职的初级律师都会被安排与一位更有经验的高级律师配对，作为正式导师计划的一部分。导师应该帮助他们的学员发展法律技能、适应律所文化，并随着时间的推移建立自己的客户资源。

正式导师计划在纸面上看起来很美好。然而在现实中，它们正越来越多地让新律师失望。

本周我得知，我曾经的律所导师——我早在2014年就被安排与她配对——去世了。显然她患有头颈部鳞状细胞癌。我很遗憾地说，直到为时已晚我才得知这个消息。

虽然这些年来我们也有许多美好的经历，但我们之间确实发生过一些冲突。我感到被利用了，变得心灰意冷。最终她离开了那家我继续工作多年的律所，而且并非好聚好散。尽管如此，我还是希望能在她临终前见她一面，也许可以告诉她一切都已释然——即使这可能是个善意的谎言。

问题是，我认为我对正式律所导师计划的总体负面体验并非个例。凭我的观察，我甚至没有见过一例在私人律所中真正蓬勃发展的正式导师-学员关系。

至少在最初阶段，许多律所学员必须依赖他们的导师获得工作。这赋予了导师对学员的巨大权力。然而，如果学员未能达到年终的计费小时要求——即使是因为他或她因为某种被认为的缺陷而被导师冷落在一旁——导师也不太可能站出来为学员承担责任。

也许在过去，当人们在一家律所开始职业生涯并在那里度过整个职业生涯时，人们有更多动力去投资培养个人。现在，随着导师、学员或双方几乎肯定会为了更好的机会而离开，承诺投入就变得困难得多。

如今，唯一能促使导师关注学员最佳利益的，只有导师本人的慷慨。你遇到过多少大型律所的合伙人真正无私？

现在，我确信有很多大型律所的合伙人在读这篇文章：你们不必给我发邮件赞扬你们的好事。我不是来评判的。人性就是如此。只是不要一本正经地试图说服我，说你们会为了帮助一个六个月前随机分配给你们的学员而牺牲自己的年终奖金。

关于律所导师计划的硬数据不容易获得。2019年的一项调查发现，只有29%的千禧一代律师受访者表示正式导师在他们的职业生涯中发挥了重要或关键作用（我敢打赌，这29%中有相当一部分人之所以这样回答，只是因为他们形成了对任何被提供的机会都要讨好导师的条件反射）。

一位研究精神病的神经科学家发现，“律师”在包含最多精神病患者的职业中排名第二，仅次于“首席执行官”。也许将一个刚从法学院毕业的单纯二十多岁的年轻人与一个在这个领域攀升至高位的随机人士配对，本身就是一个有问题的做法。

非正式的导师关系则是另一回事。我有一群非正式的学员，我称他们为朋友，我几乎每天都会收到其中一个人的电话或短信。关键在于选择。经过一段时间后，大多数人不难判断一个人是否真的关心他们。

归根结底，我认为大型律所的正式导师计划是无法修复的，至少在美国整个法律体系没有发生重大变化的情况下是如此。如果你正在阅读某家律所网站上吹嘘的所谓优秀导师计划，我建议你要持保留态度。根据自己的期望来调整预期。

对于我自己的前任导师，我希望她现在无论身在何处，都能安息。也许到了这个时候，一切真的已经释然了。

乔纳森·沃尔夫是一名民事诉讼律师，著有《你的无债务法学博士》。他教授法律写作，为各种刊物撰稿，并将财务和科学素养作为自己的事业和乐趣。他所表达的任何观点可能都是纯金般的，但仍然仅代表他本人，不应归因于任何与他有关联的机构。他也不会想要分享功劳。他的联系方式是jon_wolf@hotmail.com。

这篇文章《正式律所导师计划已濒临死亡》首次发表在Above the Law上。
articles.summary:
It’s the case at any large law firm or big government legal office, or at least it used to be, that every new associate attorney is paired up with a more experienced senior attorney as part of a formal mentorship program. Mentors are supposed to help their mentees develop their legal skills, conform to firm culture, and, over time, build their own book of business.

Formal mentorship programs look good on paper. In reality, though, they are increasingly failing new lawyers.

I learned this week that my own former law firm mentor, with whom I was first paired back in 2014, passed away. Evidently she suffered from head and neck squamous cell carcinoma. I’m sorry to say that I did not learn of this until it was too late.

Although we had a lot of good experiences over the years too, we had a bit of a falling out. I felt exploited. I became embittered. Eventually she parted ways with the firm that I continued to work at for several more years, and not amicably. Still, I wish I could have seen her before the end, maybe told her it was all water under the bridge, even if it would have been a white lie.

The thing is, I don’t think my generally negative experience with a formal law firm mentorship program was all that unique. Anecdotally, I don’t think I’ve seen even one instance of a fully thriving formal mentor-mentee relationship at a private law firm.

At least at first, many law firm mentees have to rely on their mentors for work. This gives the mentor massive power over the mentee. Yet, if the mentee fails to meet his or her billable hour requirement by the end of the year, even if it’s because he or she has been left to rot on the vine by the mentor for some perceived deficiency, it’s not like the mentor is going to step in and take the bullet for that.

Perhaps in eras past when people started at one firm and stayed their entire careers there was more incentive to invest in individuals. Now, with the mentor, mentee, or both almost sure to depart at some point for greener pastures, it’s understandably more difficult to commit.

Today, pretty much the only thing that keeps a mentor focused on the mentee’s best interests is the mentor’s own magnanimity. How many partners at big law firms have you met who are truly unselfish?

Now, I’m sure plenty of partners at big law firms are reading this right now: You don’t have to send me emails extolling your good works. I’m not here to judge. Human nature is what it is. Just don’t try to look at me with a straight face and pretend that you’d sacrifice your year-end bonus to help out an associate randomly assigned to be your mentee six months ago.

Hard data about law firm mentorship programs is not easy to come by. Apparently, a survey from 2019 found that only 29% of Millennial attorney respondents said a formal mentor had played a significant or crucial role in their career (and I’d wager that quite a few within that 29% answered as they did only from having developed a Pavlovian response to any opportunity presented to kiss their mentor’s ass).

One neuroscientist who studies psychopathy found that “lawyer” ranked second only to “CEO” as the career containing the most psychopaths. Perhaps randomly pairing a naive 20-something just out of law school with someone who’s managed to rise high in this field is an inherently dodgy prospect.

Informal mentors are another story. I’ve a collection of informal mentees, who I just call friends, and I still get a call or a text from one of them almost daily. The key there is choice. After a little time, it’s not that hard for most people (MAGA diehards notwithstanding) to figure out whether a person really gives a damn about them.

At the end of the day, I don’t think formal mentor programs at large law firms are reparable, at least absent massive changes in the entire American legal system. If you’re reading about a law firm’s supposedly great mentorship program on their website, I would advise taking that with a huge grain of salt. Set your expectations accordingly.

As for my own former mentor, I hope wherever she is now, she’s at peace. Perhaps at this point it really all is water under the bridge.

Jonathan Wolf is a civil litigator and author of Your Debt-Free JD (affiliate link). He has taught legal writing, written for a wide variety of publications, and made it both his business and his pleasure to be financially and scientifically literate. Any views he expresses are probably pure gold, but are nonetheless solely his own and should not be attributed to any organization with which he is affiliated. He wouldn’t want to share the credit anyway. He can be reached at jon_wolf@hotmail.com.

The post Formal Law Firm Mentorship Programs Are At Death’s Door appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Court Sanctions Lawyers From Both Sides In The Same Lawsuit For Filing Briefs With AI-Hallucinated Cases
articles.content:
三年前，我曾撰文报道过律师在法律文书中引用人工智能生成的虚假判例的最早案例之一。该案被法院点名批评，并面临制裁风险。然而，大多数人认为这一在法律界引发病毒式传播的新闻会起到威慑作用，但事实并非如此。过去三年间，法院对提交含有AI工具生成伪造引用的诉状的律师实施制裁的案例越来越多。

大多数此类批评都涉及诉讼一方的律师。但本案特殊——法院同时抓住了双方。

在Withers诉阿伯丁市案中，Tom Withers起诉该市违约。案件由密西西比州北区美国地方法院阿伯丁分庭审理。双方各由两名律师代理，每方各有一名本地律师和一名获准临时执业的异地律师。

某时刻，阿伯丁市提交了即决判决动议，Withers则提交了答辩状。法院在审查这些文件时，发现部分引用的法律依据无法找到。于是法院下令举行听证会，要求双方作出解释。双方随后请求法院允许提交更正的诉状。在这些文件中，临时执业律师承认使用AI起草了备忘录，且未核实所引用依据的准确性。本地律师则承认，在提交前未检查联合代理律师备忘录的准确性。

听证会上，所有涉事律师普遍承认未能核实其签署的备忘录中的引用，并接受了责任。但其中两名律师的解释值得详述。

一名律师对法院的命令感到震惊，声称她不知道AI会引用虚假判例，也不知道什么是“幻觉判例”。法院认为这一解释不充分且令人难以置信。法院随后指出，这名律师在收到法院通知后仍继续提交由AI生成且未经核实的备忘录。这导致她在路易斯安那州破产法院又受到一次制裁——因提交了两份含有AI幻觉判例法的备忘录。

另一名涉事临时执业律师则表示，她使用的AI软件是专为内部法律研究设计的，仅使用了90天，且该软件是根据她执业的州定制的，而非密西西比州。“内部法律研究”这一术语具体指办公室用途的法律研究工具，还是专为内部企业律师定制的软件，目前尚不清楚。她还承认这是她第一次处理密西西比州的案件。

综合考虑各项事实和律师的解释，法院对所有涉事律师施加了制裁。对于两名仅签署备忘录的本地律师，法院命令每人支付1000美元。两名临时执业律师则分别被制裁2500美元和3500美元。

此外，所有涉事律师均被取消代理资格。临时执业律师的执业许可被撤销，并被禁止在两年内申请在该法院执业。法院还将把制裁令的副本发送给每位律师所在州的律师协会，由他们决定适当的回应。

法官同时取消了审判。

虽然这可能是一个有趣的案例，可以用AI对抗AI来比较哪一方在动议起草方面更胜一筹，但现实是，即使过了好几年，AI仍然无法正确引用判例。如果公众看到这些案例，不仅会对法律行业更加失去尊重，还会抱着试试看的心态使用AI，用它来编造更多关于律师的笑话。

我没有点名涉事律师，原因如下。首先，被Above the Law提及会在互联网上留下重大痕迹，我认为他们目前以及可能未来面临的专业后果已经足够严重。重要的教训是，法院和州律师协会将对不能明智使用AI的律师采取更严厉的态度。其次，我想知道为什么一些律师愿意拿自己的职业声誉冒险，在不核实准确性的情况下提交AI生成的法律备忘录。我希望听到并匿名分享他们的故事，让其他人从他们的错误中吸取教训。我认为不能简单地将他们的行为归因于懒惰。如果您想分享您的（或您“朋友”的）故事，请给我发电子邮件。

Gavel Bang：感谢Rob Freund发现这一案例。

Steven Chung是加利福尼亚州洛杉矶市的税务律师，帮助人们进行基本税务规划并解决税务争议。他也对背负大量学生贷款的人表示同情。他可以通过电子邮件stevenchungatl@gmail.com联系他。您也可以在Twitter（@stevenchung）上关注他，或在LinkedIn上与他联系。

本文首先发表于Above the Law，标题为《法院对同案双方律师提交含有AI幻觉判例的诉状实施制裁》。
articles.summary:
Three years ago, I wrote about one of the first instances of a lawyer citing fake cases generate by artificial intelligence (AI) in their legal briefs only to have the court call them out on it and consider sanctions. While most would think that the news that went viral in the legal community would serve as a deterrent to those tempted, it has not. Over the past three years, courts have increasingly sanctioned attorneys for submitting briefs containing fabricated citations generated by AI tools.

Most of these benchslaps involved an attorney on one side of a lawsuit. But this is a case where the court caught both sides redhanded.

In Withers v. City of Aberdeen, Tom Withers sued the city for breach of contract. The venue is the United States District Court for the Northern District of Mississippi, Aberdeen Division. Each side was represented by two attorneys with each side having one local counsel and an outside counsel admitted pro hac vice.

At one point, Aberdeen filed a motion for summary judgment, and Withers filed an opposition. When the court reviewed the filings, it could not locate some of the cited legal authorities. So the court ordered a hearing to demand explanations from both sides. Each side then asked the court’s permission to file corrected briefs. In those filings, the pro hac vice attorneys admitted to drafting the memorandums using AI and not cite checking the authorities cited. The local attorneys admitted that they did not check their co-counsel’s memorandums for accuracy before filing them.

At the hearing, all of the attorneys involved generally admitted that they failed to cite-check the memorandums they signed off on and accepted responsibility. But two of the attorneys’ explanations are worth mentioning.

One attorney was shocked at the court’s order and claimed that she did not know that AI can cite false cases and didn’t know what a hallucinated case was. The court found this explanation to be insufficient and incredulous. The court then stated that this attorney continued to file AI-generated and unverified memorandums after she received notice from the court. This resulted in another sanction from the Louisiana Bankruptcy Court for filing two memorandums with AI-hallucinated case law.

The other pro hac vice attorney involved stated that she used AI software that was built for in-house legal research, only used it for 90 days, and that it was custom made to produce results from the state where she practices but not from Mississippi. It is not clear whether the term “in-house legal research” means an office-use legal research tool or if it is software specifically tailored to in-house corporate attorneys. She also admitted to this being her first time handling a Mississippi case.

After considering the facts and the explanations from the attorneys, the court imposed sanctions on all of the attorneys involved. For the two local counsel who simply signed off the memorandums, they were each ordered to pay $1,000. The two pro hac vice counsel were sanctioned $2,500 and $3,500.

Also, every attorney involved has been disqualified from the case. The pro hac vice attorneys have had their admissions revoked and are banned from seeking admissions to the court for two years. The court will also send a copy of its sanctions order to the state bars where each of the attorneys are practicing where they will decide the appropriate response.

The judge also cancelled the trial.

While this could have been an interesting way to pit AI vs. AI to see which is better at motion drafting, it remains that AI cannot cite cases correctly even after several years. If the general public sees this, they will not only lose more respect towards the profession, but they will try their luck with AI and use it to conjure up more lawyer jokes.

I did not name the attorneys involved for several reasons. First, being mentioned on Above the Law leaves a big mark on the internet, and I think the professional repercussions they suffered now and possibly later is enough. The important lesson is that courts and state bars will get tougher on attorneys who do not wisely use AI. Second, I would like to know why some attorneys are taking a risk on their professional reputation by submitting AI-generated legal memoranda without verifying accuracy. I would like to hear and anonymously share their stories so that others will learn from their mistakes. I think it is not enough to attribute their behavior to pure laziness. If you want to share your (or your “friend’s”) story, please send me an email.

Gavel Bang: Rob Freund for finding this gem.

Steven Chung is a tax attorney in Los Angeles, California. He helps people with basic tax planning and resolve tax disputes. He is also sympathetic to people with large student loans. He can be reached via email at stevenchungatl@gmail.com. Or you can connect with him on Twitter (@stevenchung) and connect with him on LinkedIn.

The post Court Sanctions Lawyers From Both Sides In The Same Lawsuit For Filing Briefs With AI-Hallucinated Cases appeared first on Above the Law.
